title: Robert Anderson's Electric Carriage
body: Scottish inventor Robert Anderson builds a crude electric carriage powered by non-rechargeable primary cells. It is the first known electric vehicle, but with no way to recharge the batteries it remains a curiosity, not transportation.

title: Thomas Davenport Builds the First American DC Motor
body: Vermont blacksmith Thomas Davenport and his wife Emily construct the first American direct-current electric motor. A year later he uses it to drive a small electric car around a circular track — the first electric railway. He patents the design in 1837.

title: Robert Davidson's 'Galvani' Electric Locomotive
body: Scotsman Robert Davidson runs a battery-powered locomotive named Galvani on the Edinburgh-Glasgow line. The 7-ton machine reaches 4 mph but burns through expensive single-use zinc-acid batteries. Steam workers — fearing for their jobs — destroy it.

title: Gaston Planté Invents the Rechargeable Battery
body: French physicist Gaston Planté builds the first practical rechargeable battery — the lead-acid cell — using two lead sheets separated by rubber, immersed in sulfuric acid. It's the breakthrough that makes electric vehicles actually possible.

title: William Morrison's Electric Wagon in Iowa
body: Chemist William Morrison debuts a six-passenger electric wagon in a Des Moines parade. It tops out around 14 mph on 24 lead-acid cells and is widely considered the first successful electric vehicle in the United States.
source: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

title: London's 'Hummingbird' Electric Cabs
body: Walter Bersey launches a fleet of 12 electric hackney cabs on London's streets — nicknamed 'Hummingbirds' for the sound they made. The fleet grows to 75 cabs, but heavy weight chews through tires and batteries; the service collapses by 1899.

title: New York City's First Electric Taxi Fleet
body: The Samuel's Electric Carriage and Wagon Company puts 12 electric hansom cabs on New York streets. Within a year the fleet grows to 62 cabs. The operation is absorbed into the Electric Vehicle Company, which would run roughly 100 NYC taxis.

title: Baker Motor Vehicle Company Founded
body: Baker Electric is founded in Cleveland, Ohio. By 1906 it is the largest electric car maker in the US, building 800 cars per year. Thomas Edison himself drives a Baker — calling it the perfect vehicle.

title: EVs Rule the American Road
body: At the dawn of the auto age, electric cars are winning. Of 4,192 US automobiles counted in the 1900 Census, 38% are electric, 40% are steam, and just 22% are gasoline. Electric cars are quiet, clean, and require no hand-cranking — beloved especially by city drivers.
source: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

title: Detroit Electric Begins Production
body: The Anderson Electric Car Company starts building Detroit Electrics, which become the longest-lasting EV brand of the era — over 13,000 sold by 1939. With an 80-mile range, they are favorites of Clara Ford and Mrs. Thomas Edison.
source: Edison Tech Center: History of Electric Cars

title: Ford Launches the Model T
body: Henry Ford's Model T arrives at $850, plunging to $260 by the 1920s thanks to assembly-line mass production. Cheap, simple gasoline transport is suddenly within reach of working families — the first existential threat to the electric car.
source: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

title: The Electric Self-Starter Kills the Crank
body: Cadillac introduces Charles Kettering's electric self-starter, ending the era of dangerous hand-cranking gasoline engines. The single biggest selling point of electric cars — easy starting — evaporates overnight. (First demonstrated in 1911 and fitted to the 1912 Cadillac, it ended the dangerous hand-crank — and with it the electric car's biggest selling point.)

title: Edison and Ford's Electric Car That Never Was
body: The New York Times confirms Henry Ford and Thomas Edison are collaborating on a cheap electric car using Edison's nickel-iron batteries. Ford reportedly loans Edison $1.2M for 100,000 batteries. But the partnership fizzles and the car never reaches market.

title: Cheap Texas Oil Buries the EV
body: With Texas crude flowing and gas stations spreading along new highways, gasoline cars get cheaper, faster, and longer-range. By the late 1920s electric vehicles have all but disappeared from American roads.
source: US Department of Energy: History of the Electric Car

title: The Henney Kilowatt — An EV Before Its Time
body: Built on a Renault Dauphine body, the Henney Kilowatt is America's first modern production electric car. Only 47 are built across 1959-60; most go to electric utilities. With a 40-mile range and 30 mph top speed, it can't beat the gas car. The project folds.

title: GM's Electrovair and Electrovan
body: GM unveils the Electrovair II — an electric Chevrolet Corvair — and the Electrovan, the world's first hydrogen fuel cell vehicle. Both are experimental. The Electrovan's platinum-laden fuel cells cost so much GM jokes they could 'buy a fleet of vans.'

title: OPEC Embargo Sends US Back to EVs
body: The Arab Oil Embargo quadruples oil prices and lines snake around US gas stations. Suddenly electric vehicle research gets 'apocalyptic urgency and essentially unlimited funding.' Every Detroit automaker reopens EV programs.

title: Sebring-Vanguard CitiCar Hits the Road
body: Built in Sebring, Florida, the boxy little CitiCar tops 30 mph with a 40-mile range. About 2,300 are sold, making Sebring-Vanguard briefly the sixth-largest US automaker by 1975 — and holding the US EV production record until the Tesla Model S.

title: Congress Passes the EV R&D Act Over Ford's Veto
body: Congress overrides President Ford's veto to enact the Electric and Hybrid Vehicle Research, Development, and Demonstration Act — a 5-year, $160M federal program to develop a practical EV and put 7,500 demonstration vehicles on the road.

title: GM Unveils the Impact at the LA Auto Show
body: GM Chairman Roger Smith unveils the Impact, a sleek electric concept developed with AeroVironment. Sensing momentum, California's Air Resources Board responds with a Zero-Emission Vehicle mandate: 2% of major automakers' fleet must be ZEV by 1998.

title: GM EV1 Deliveries Begin
body: GM begins delivering the EV1 — the first mass-produced electric car from a major automaker in nearly 90 years. Lease-only at $399-$549/month, available only in California and Arizona. Just over 1,100 are built before the program is killed.

title: Toyota Prius Launches in Japan
body: Toyota launches the first-generation Prius — the world's first mass-produced hybrid passenger car. It sells 3,500 units in its first month and wins Japan's Car of the Year for 1998. The Prius makes 'hybrid' a household word.

title: GM Kills the EV1 — and Crushes the Cars
body: GM cancels the EV1 program, refuses to renew leases, and recalls every car. Despite a $1.9M offer from drivers to buy them back, GM sends almost all EV1s to be crushed. The destruction becomes a rallying cry for EV advocates.
source: Wikipedia: Who Killed the Electric Car?

title: Tesla Motors Founded
body: Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning incorporate Tesla Motors in San Carlos, California — named after Nikola Tesla. They are inspired in part by GM's destruction of the EV1, betting that lithium-ion can finally make a fast, practical electric car.

title: 'Who Killed the Electric Car?' Hits Theaters
body: Chris Paine's documentary, narrated by Martin Sheen, tells the story of the GM EV1's creation and destruction. It indicts oil companies, automakers, CARB, and the federal government — and turns EVs into a cultural cause.
source: Wikipedia: Who Killed the Electric Car?

title: First Tesla Roadster Delivered
body: Tesla delivers Roadster #0001 to co-founder Ian Wright. With a Lotus chassis, a lithium-ion battery, and a 0-60 in under 4 seconds, the Roadster proves an electric car can be sexy and fast. About 2,450 are built through 2012.

title: Nissan Leaf and Chevy Volt Reach Customers
body: On the same December weekend, the first Nissan Leaf (all-electric) is delivered in California and the first Chevy Volts (plug-in hybrid) leave a Detroit plant. The mass-market EV era officially begins.

title: Tesla Model S Begins Customer Deliveries
body: Tesla delivers the first Model S sedans at its Fremont factory. With 200+ miles of range, OTA software updates, and a 17-inch touchscreen, it transforms the EV from compliance car to status symbol. Motor Trend names it 2013 Car of the Year.

title: Model 3 Unveiled — 400,000 Preorders
body: Tesla unveils the $35,000 Model 3 in Hawthorne, California. Within 24 hours, 115,000 people put down $1,000 deposits sight-unseen. Within two weeks reservations approach 400,000 — the biggest product launch in auto history.

title: Model 3 Production Begins
body: Tesla starts shipping the Model 3 in late 2017, kicking off what Elon Musk calls 'production hell.' By 2018 it's outselling every other EV in the world and becomes the bestselling premium sedan in the US.

title: Tesla Model Y Launches + Shanghai Surge
body: Tesla begins delivering the Model Y crossover in early 2020. Meanwhile its new Shanghai gigafactory ramps Model 3 production for the China market — China explodes from niche to the world's largest EV market.

title: Rivian R1T Beats Tesla to Electric Trucks
body: Startup Rivian delivers the first R1T — the first electric pickup truck in the US market — months before Ford. Eight months later, in May 2022, Ford begins delivering the F-150 Lightning, electrifying America's bestselling vehicle.

title: Biden Signs the Inflation Reduction Act
body: The IRA — the largest climate law in US history — restructures the federal EV tax credit. Credits of up to $7,500 now require North American final assembly, plus battery sourcing rules that reshape global supply chains.

title: Norway Hits 79% EV Market Share
body: In 2022 nearly 4 in 5 new cars sold in Norway are electric — 79.3% counting BEVs plus PHEVs (82.4% pure battery-electric over the year). Norway proves a developed economy can flip its new-car market to electric in a decade.

title: BYD Overtakes Tesla in Quarterly BEV Sales
body: For the first time, Chinese automaker BYD outsells Tesla in a single quarter — 595,413 battery-electric vehicles to Tesla's 484,507. Tesla still wins the full year (1.8M vs 1.57M), but the Chinese EV industry has clearly arrived.
stat: BYD 595k vs Tesla 485k BEVs in Q4

title: Global EV Sales Hit ~14 Million
body: Roughly 14 million electric cars (BEV+PHEV) are sold worldwide in 2023 — nearly 1 in 5 new cars sold globally. China accounts for about 60% of registrations. The world has crossed the EV tipping point.

title: Battery Prices Fall to $115/kWh
body: BloombergNEF reports lithium-ion battery pack prices fall 20% in 2024 to a global average of $115/kWh — the biggest one-year drop since 2017. In China, packs fall to $94/kWh. EV battery prices drop below $100/kWh for the first time.

title: EVs Cross 20% of Global New-Car Sales
body: Global electric car sales exceed 17 million in 2024, with EVs accounting for more than 20% of the worldwide new-car market for the first time. The internal combustion engine, after a 120-year reign, is now visibly losing ground.
